REFERENCE: The components of this citation have multiple inconsistencies. The journal 'Cell' is real, but the volume number (374) is implausible for a 2021 issue—Cell's actual volume numbers in 2021 were around 184. The article number format (e704479) resembles use in the European Journal of Cell Biology, not typically Cell. The DOI prefix (10.9045) does not correspond to any recognized publisher (Cell Press/Elsevier uses 10.1016). The date format and author formatting (trailing period after 'Lane, R..') are also non-standard. No evidence exists that this article was published in Cell. This citation appears to be fabricated.
authors: The author is listed as 'Lane, R..' which includes an extra trailing period after the initial. While 'R. Lane' could refer to a plausible author name, the formatting is non-standard and the extra period suggests a formatting error or fabrication.
title: The title 'Obligations in a global health emergency' sounds plausible as a medical/ethics topic, but there is no evidence this specific article exists in Cell or any other journal under this exact title. The title is likely fabricated.
journal_name: 'Cell' is a real and well-known scientific journal published by Cell Press (an imprint of Elsevier). The journal name itself is correct.
journal_volume: Volume 374 in 2021 is implausible for Cell. Cell's volume numbering in 2021 was approximately 184. The volume number is clearly incorrect.
journal_issue: The issue number 7189 is inconsistent with Cell's issue numbering system. Cell issues typically number roughly 1–52 per volume. An issue number of 7189 is fabricated.
journal_page: Cell does not use page numbers in the traditional sense for many modern articles, relying on article numbers instead. No page range is provided here, which could be acceptable, but the overall format is incorrect for Cell.
elocator: The elocator/article number 'e704479' with an 'e' prefix is not typical for Cell journal, which uses different article numbering conventions.
publication_date: The date '2021 Dec 10' is written in an unusual format (year month day with month abbreviated and day appended). Standard Vancouver formatting would use just the year or a different date format. While the date itself isn't obviously impossible, its formatting is non-standard and contributes to the suspicion of fabrication.
doi: The DOI prefix 10.9045 does not correspond to any known publisher registry. A legitimate DOI for Cell would use the prefix 10.1016 (Elsevier/Cell Press). The DOI string '10.9045.52/yeci-o8a4' does not follow standard DOI conventions for the journal in question and does not resolve to a real article.
